description: Jet Bridge perspective — the walkway from Terminal to Cockpit. Tutorials on prompt craft, context management, edit mode, and agent mode patterns.

promptCraft.title: Prompt Craft — Talking to Copilot
promptCraft.intro: Copilot is only as good as the context you give it. A vague prompt gets a vague answer. A precise prompt — with intent, constraints, and examples — gets production-ready code. These techniques apply across all surfaces: completions, chat, edits, and agent mode.

techniques.title: Be Specific About Intent
techniques.good: // Wrap the API call in try/catch. On 429, retry with exponential backoff (max 3 retries). On 5xx, log the error and return a fallback response.
techniques.why: Copilot uses your comment as the spec. More detail means fewer iterations.

techniques.title: Provide Input/Output Examples
techniques.good:
// Parse date strings like '2026-04-13', 'April 13, 2026', or '13/04/2026' into ISO 8601 format.
// Example: 'April 13, 2026' → '2026-04-13'
techniques.why: Concrete examples disambiguate format expectations and edge cases.

techniques.title: Name Things Well
techniques.why: Copilot reads your function name, parameter names, and type annotations to infer what to generate. Descriptive names act as implicit prompts.

techniques.title: Keep Relevant Tabs Open
techniques.bad: Work with only the current file open
techniques.good: Open related files (types, tests, similar implementations) as tabs in your editor
techniques.why: Copilot uses open tabs as context. A test file open alongside your implementation helps Copilot understand the expected behavior.

techniques.title: Iterate, Don't Restart
techniques.bad: Delete Copilot's suggestion and retype your prompt from scratch
techniques.good: Accept the partial suggestion, then add a follow-up comment refining what's missing
techniques.why: Each accepted line becomes context for the next suggestion. Building incrementally is faster than starting over.

techniques.title: Use Type Annotations
techniques.why: Type signatures tell Copilot the exact shape of inputs and outputs. In TypeScript and Python (with type hints), this dramatically improves suggestion quality.

contextManagement.title: Context Management — Scoping What Copilot Sees
contextManagement.intro: Copilot Chat offers participants and variables that control what context is sent with your prompt. Mastering these is the difference between generic answers and codebase-aware solutions.

participants.name: @workspace
participants.description: Searches your entire workspace using embeddings and ranks relevant files. Best for broad questions about architecture, patterns, or finding code.
participants.example: @workspace How does authentication work in this project?
participants.tip: Use @workspace for discovery. Once you know which files matter, switch to #file for precision.

participants.name: @vscode
participants.description: Asks about VS Code settings, keybindings, and extension APIs. Useful when configuring your editor.
participants.example: @vscode How do I change the font size in the terminal?
participants.tip: This participant knows VS Code internals — use it for editor questions, not code questions.

participants.name: @terminal
participants.description: Has context about your integrated terminal — recent commands, output, and errors.
participants.tip: Great for debugging build failures or test errors without copy-pasting terminal output.

variables.name: #file
variables.description: Attaches a specific file to the chat context. Pick the exact file Copilot should read.
variables.tip: More precise than @workspace when you know which file contains the answer.

variables.name: #selection
variables.description: Sends your current editor selection as context.
variables.example: Refactor #selection to use async/await instead of callbacks
variables.tip: Select the code first, then reference it in chat for targeted refactoring.

variables.name: #codebase
variables.description: Like @workspace but as a variable — searches the full codebase for relevant context.
variables.example: Find all usages of the UserService class #codebase
variables.tip: Use when you want codebase search combined with other participants.

editMode.title: Edit Mode — Targeted Multi-File Changes
editMode.intro: Edit mode sits between inline completions and agent mode. You describe a change in natural language, Copilot proposes diffs across one or more files, and you review before applying. No terminal commands, no autonomous actions — just precise edits with human review.

workflows.title: Inline Edit (Ctrl+I)
steps: Describe the change in natural language
Review the inline diff and accept or reject
workflows.bestFor: Quick, scoped changes to a single block of code
workflows.example: Select a function → Ctrl+I → 'Add null checks for all parameters and throw descriptive errors'

workflows.title: Edits Panel
steps: Open Copilot Edits (Ctrl+Shift+I → switch to Edits)
Add files to the working set
Review proposed diffs across all files
workflows.bestFor: Multi-file refactors where you want to review every change before applying
workflows.example: Add working set: model.ts, controller.ts, routes.ts → 'Add a deletedAt soft-delete field and filter it in all queries'

workflows.title: Apply from Chat
steps: When Copilot suggests code, click 'Apply in Editor'
Review the diff overlay in the editor
workflows.bestFor: When a chat conversation produces code you want to use immediately
workflows.example: Ask 'How would I add rate limiting to this Express router?' → Apply the suggested middleware

agentPatterns.title: Agent Mode — Autonomous Workflows
agentPatterns.intro: Agent mode is Copilot's most powerful surface. It can read files, edit code, run terminal commands, and iterate on errors — all autonomously. But with great power comes the need for clear instructions and careful review.

patterns.title: Ask for a Plan First
patterns.description: Before letting the agent execute, ask it to outline its approach. Review the plan, adjust scope, then approve execution.
patterns.example: I need to migrate from Express to Fastify. Don't make changes yet — first outline which files need to change and what the migration steps are.

patterns.title: Set Clear Boundaries
patterns.description: Tell the agent what NOT to change. Explicit exclusions prevent scope creep.
patterns.example: Add input validation to the user registration endpoint. Don't modify the database schema or change any existing tests.

patterns.title: Test-Driven Agent Work
patterns.description: Give the agent a failing test and ask it to make it pass. The test becomes both specification and verification.
patterns.example: The test in tests/auth.spec.ts line 42 is failing. Read the test, understand what it expects, and fix the implementation to make it pass.

patterns.title: Iterative Review Cycles
patterns.description: Don't approve everything at once. Review each terminal command and file change before the agent continues.
patterns.example: After each file change, pause and show me a summary of what you changed and why before continuing to the next file.

patterns.title: Use Git as Your Safety Net
patterns.description: Commit before starting agent work. If the agent goes off track, you can always git stash or reset.
patterns.example: Before we start: commit all current changes. Then create a new branch 'feature/agent-refactor' for this work.

nextSteps.title: Ready for Boarding — Next Steps
nextSteps.intro: You've walked the jet bridge. You know how to talk to Copilot, scope its context, use edit mode for precision, and agent mode for autonomy. Now board the aircraft:

destinations.reason: See all 48 instruments and start your daily Copilot workflow
Understand the threat model and how Copilot handles your code
Deep dive into Agent Mode, MCP servers, and the Cloud Agent
Explore which AI models power each Copilot surface
Admin controls, governance policies, and sovereign cloud options
